ClinicalDocument.title: Continuity of Care Document - Benjamin Smith

value.displayName: for persistent vomiting to enable oral rehydration
if amoxicillin failure or recent antibiotic use

act.text: Evaluation for gerd symptoms GERD, newly diagnosed
- [medication] Continue current medication regimen - [follow-up] Return in 3-6 months for condition review
Chronic Cough follow-up Chronic Cough, stable
- [medication] Continue current medication regimen - [follow-up] Return in 3-6 months for condition review
Well-child examination - 13 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit
Obesity follow-up Obesity, stable
- [education] Nutrition counseling; goal of modest lifestyle changes - [referral] Refer to dietitian for comprehensive nutrition plan - [follow-up] Return in 3 months for weight check
Chronic Cough follow-up Chronic Cough, stable
- [medication] Continue current medication regimen - [follow-up] Return in 3-6 months for condition review

"He woke up yesterday morning with a fever around 101. By afternoon it got up to 103 and he's been complaining his head and body ache. We gave him ibuprofen and it comes down a bit, but then it goes back up. He's not eating much and just wants to lie in bed. I'm worried because he's never had a fever this high before."

Fever: Clinical presentation of fever with normal vital signs (HR 77) and unremarkable exam (normocephalic, clear lungs bilaterally) suggests self-limited viral illness; further workup indicated if fever persists or constitutional symptoms develop.
- [medication] Acetaminophen or ibuprofen for temperature control - [education] Monitor for source of infection, return if fever persists >3 days - [follow-up] Follow up as needed if symptoms worsen
Obesity follow-up Obesity, stable
- [education] Nutrition counseling; goal of modest lifestyle changes - [referral] Refer to dietitian for comprehensive nutrition plan - [follow-up] Return in 3 months for weight check
Chronic Cough follow-up Chronic Cough, stable
- [medication] Continue current medication regimen - [follow-up] Return in 3-6 months for condition review
Well-child examination - 12 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit
Chronic Cough follow-up Chronic Cough, stable
- [medication] Continue current medication regimen - [follow-up] Return in 3-6 months for condition review
Obesity follow-up Obesity, stable
- [education] Nutrition counseling; goal of modest lifestyle changes - [referral] Refer to dietitian for comprehensive nutrition plan - [follow-up] Return in 3 months for weight check

"He woke up yesterday morning with a runny nose and complained his throat felt scratchy. By afternoon he had a fever—we took his temperature and it was 101. He's been sneezy and has a lot of clear mucus, but no cough yet. We gave him some ibuprofen and he's been drinking okay, but I'm worried because he never gets fevers like this and I want to make sure it's not strep or something more serious."

Upper Respiratory Infection: Clinical presentation consistent with viral URI: fever, clear rhinorrhea with mild nasal mucosa erythema, and mild oropharyngeal erythema without exudate support upper respiratory involvement; clear lungs exclude lower respiratory tract infection.

- [medication] Acetaminophen 500mg Q4-6H PRN PRN - [medication] Ibuprofen 340mg Q6-8H PRN PRN - [education] Supportive care with rest and increased fluids - [education] Saline nasal drops and bulb suction for congestion - [education] Honey (if >1 year) for cough relief - [education] Return if: difficulty breathing, fever >3 days, ear pain, or worsening symptoms

"He started throwing up yesterday morning, maybe around 10 o'clock. At first I thought it was just one time, but then he kept going back to the bathroom. He's had diarrhea too, and this morning I noticed he had a fever—I checked it a couple times and it's been around 100 to 101. He won't eat anything and just wants to lay on the couch. I've been giving him water and some Gatorade, but I'm worried he's not keeping enough down. He seems pretty tired, which isn't like him."

Viral Gastroenteritis: Clinical presentation consistent w/ viral gastroenteritis: fever, tachycardia, and mild dehydration (dry mucous membranes) support infectious gastroenteritis etiology; normal pulmonary and HEENT exams exclude alternative diagnoses (URI, pneumonia).

- [medication] Ondansetron 5mg x1 dose PRN PRN - [education] Oral rehydration with small frequent amounts (1-2 tsp every 5 minutes) - [education] Use Pedialyte or similar oral rehydration solution - [education] Advance diet as tolerated - no need for BRAT restriction - [education] Return if: unable to keep fluids down, no urine in 8 hours, blood in stool, or appears very ill
Chronic Cough follow-up Chronic Cough, stable
- [medication] Continue current medication regimen - [follow-up] Return in 3-6 months for condition review

HPI (Parent Narrative): Benjamin woke up yesterday morning with a sore throat and has had a fever on and off since then—we've been checking it with our thermometer at home. He's been complaining that his head hurts and just wants to lie around, which isn't like him. We gave him some ibuprofen and he's been drinking water okay, but he won't eat much and seems pretty tired. We're mainly worried it might be something serious, so we wanted to get him checked out.

Viral Syndrome: Clinical presentation consistent w/ viral syndrome: mild tachycardia (HR 102) likely d/t systemic viral infection; normal HEENT & clear lungs b/l effectively r/o bacterial sinusitis, otitis media, & lower respiratory tract infection.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days
Obesity follow-up Obesity, stable
- [education] Nutrition counseling; goal of modest lifestyle changes - [referral] Refer to dietitian for comprehensive nutrition plan - [follow-up] Return in 3 months for weight check

"He woke up yesterday morning complaining that he felt hot and achy all over. His temperature was 101.5 at home, and he's been running a fever on and off since then, even with Tylenol every four hours. He's not interested in eating much and just wants to lay around, which isn't like him at all. We're mostly worried because he has soccer tournament coming up this weekend and want to make sure it's nothing serious."

Fever: History and exam consistent w/ fever: Pt presents w/ elevated temp; vital signs show appropriate HR response (90 bpm); HEENT and pulmonary exams unremarkable, making serious systemic infection less likely at this time.
- [medication] Acetaminophen or ibuprofen for temperature control - [education] Monitor for source of infection, return if fever persists >3 days - [follow-up] Follow up as needed if symptoms worsen

HPI (Parent Narrative): Benjamin woke up yesterday morning with a sore throat and has had a low-grade fever since then, ranging from about 100 to 101.5. He's been complaining that his head hurts and says he feels tired, though he's still drinking fluids okay. We gave him some ibuprofen and he's been resting at home, but the fever keeps coming back when the medicine wears off, so we wanted to get him checked out to make sure it's nothing serious.

Viral Syndrome: Clinical presentation consistent with viral syndrome: mild tachycardia (HR 101) likely secondary to systemic infection, with normal HEENT and clear lungs ruling out localized bacterial infection or lower respiratory involvement.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days
Chronic Cough follow-up Chronic Cough, stable
- [medication] Continue current medication regimen - [follow-up] Return in 3-6 months for condition review
Obesity follow-up Obesity, stable
- [education] Nutrition counseling; goal of modest lifestyle changes - [referral] Refer to dietitian for comprehensive nutrition plan - [follow-up] Return in 3 months for weight check
Well-child examination - 11 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit
Chronic Cough follow-up Chronic Cough, stable
- [medication] Continue current medication regimen - [follow-up] Return in 3-6 months for condition review
Obesity follow-up Obesity, stable
- [education] Nutrition counseling; goal of modest lifestyle changes - [referral] Refer to dietitian for comprehensive nutrition plan - [follow-up] Return in 3 months for weight check
Chronic Cough follow-up Chronic Cough, stable
- [medication] Continue current medication regimen - [follow-up] Return in 3-6 months for condition review

"He woke up yesterday morning with a fever, and it's been pretty high—we checked it this morning and it was 103.5. He's been complaining his head and body ache, and he's just really tired, won't eat much even though I made his favorite lunch. We gave him some ibuprofen and that brought it down for a bit, but it keeps coming back. I'm worried because he never gets fevers like this, and I want to make sure it's nothing serious."

Fever: Clinical presentation of fever with normal vital signs (HR 86) and unremarkable exam (normocephalic, clear lungs) suggests benign viral etiology; further workup indicated if fever persists or constitutional symptoms develop.
- [medication] Acetaminophen or ibuprofen for temperature control - [education] Monitor for source of infection, return if fever persists >3 days - [follow-up] Follow up as needed if symptoms worsen
Obesity follow-up Obesity, stable
- [education] Nutrition counseling; goal of modest lifestyle changes - [referral] Refer to dietitian for comprehensive nutrition plan - [follow-up] Return in 3 months for weight check

"His left eye started getting red and goopy yesterday morning. At first I thought he just had sleep in his eye, but it got worse throughout the day—now it's swollen and he says it's itchy and uncomfortable. He's been rubbing it a lot even though I told him not to. I've been rinsing it with warm water and he's been wearing his glasses instead of contacts, but it doesn't seem to be getting better. I'm worried it might be an infection and want to make sure he can go back to school soon since he has soccer practice this weekend."

Conjunctivitis: History and exam consistent w/ conjunctivitis: bilateral conjunctival injection and discharge noted on HEENT exam w/ preserved visual acuity and clear cornea, excluding more serious anterior segment pathology. Vitals stable (HR 76) w/ clear lungs, suggesting likely infectious etiology w/o systemic involvement.

- [medication] Erythromycin Ophthalmic Ointment per package Apply 0.5 inch ribbon QID x 7 days - [education] Antibiotic drops for bacterial; supportive care for viral - [education] Good hand hygiene to prevent spread - [education] Do not share towels, pillowcases, or eye makeup - [education] May return to school 24 hours after starting treatment if bacterial - [education] Return if: vision changes, severe pain, or photophobia
Obesity follow-up Obesity, stable
- [education] Nutrition counseling; goal of modest lifestyle changes - [referral] Refer to dietitian for comprehensive nutrition plan - [follow-up] Return in 3 months for weight check
Chronic Cough follow-up Chronic Cough, stable
- [medication] Continue current medication regimen - [follow-up] Return in 3-6 months for condition review
Well-child examination - 10 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit
Obesity follow-up Obesity, stable
- [education] Nutrition counseling; goal of modest lifestyle changes - [referral] Refer to dietitian for comprehensive nutrition plan - [follow-up] Return in 3 months for weight check
Chronic Cough follow-up Chronic Cough, stable
- [medication] Continue current medication regimen - [follow-up] Return in 3-6 months for condition review
Chronic Cough follow-up Chronic Cough, stable
- [medication] Continue current medication regimen - [follow-up] Return in 3-6 months for condition review
Obesity follow-up Obesity, stable
- [education] Nutrition counseling; goal of modest lifestyle changes - [referral] Refer to dietitian for comprehensive nutrition plan - [follow-up] Return in 3 months for weight check

"He woke up yesterday morning with some red bumps on his chest and back. By this afternoon they had spread to his arms and legs, and he's been scratching at them constantly. We gave him some calamine lotion and children's Benadryl last night, but it doesn't seem to be helping much. I'm worried it might be contagious—I'm not sure if he should go back to school."

Rash: History and exam findings of rash with normal vital signs (HR 94) and unremarkable systemic exam (clear lungs, normal HEENT) support benign dermatologic process without evidence of systemic involvement or acute illness.
- [other] Topical care as appropriate for rash type - [education] Return if rash spreads, becomes painful, or child develops fever
Well-child examination - 9 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit
Chronic Cough follow-up Chronic Cough, stable
- [medication] Continue current medication regimen - [follow-up] Return in 3-6 months for condition review
Obesity follow-up Obesity, stable
- [education] Nutrition counseling; goal of modest lifestyle changes - [referral] Refer to dietitian for comprehensive nutrition plan - [follow-up] Return in 3 months for weight check

"His left eye started getting red and goopy yesterday morning. At first I thought he just had sleep in his eye, but it got worse throughout the day—now both eyes are red and he's got yellow discharge, especially when he wakes up. He's been rubbing at them a lot and says they're itchy and uncomfortable. I've been cleaning them with a warm washcloth and tried some saline drops this morning, but it's not getting better. I'm worried it might be something that needs antibiotics or if he'll be able to go back to school."

Conjunctivitis: History and exam findings support viral conjunctivitis: bilateral conjunctival injection with discharge, preauricular lymphadenopathy (classic viral sign), and clear cornea with normal VA. Absence of purulent exudate and systemic toxicity further support viral etiology over bacterial cause.

- [medication] Erythromycin Ophthalmic Ointment per package Apply 0.5 inch ribbon QID x 7 days - [education] Antibiotic drops for bacterial; supportive care for viral - [education] Good hand hygiene to prevent spread - [education] Do not share towels, pillowcases, or eye makeup - [education] May return to school 24 hours after starting treatment if bacterial - [education] Return if: vision changes, severe pain, or photophobia
Chronic Cough follow-up Chronic Cough, stable
- [medication] Continue current medication regimen - [follow-up] Return in 3-6 months for condition review
Obesity follow-up Obesity, stable
- [education] Nutrition counseling; goal of modest lifestyle changes - [referral] Refer to dietitian for comprehensive nutrition plan - [follow-up] Return in 3 months for weight check

"His left eye started getting red and watery yesterday morning. Today it's gotten worse—there's some yellowish crusty stuff in the corner and he keeps rubbing at it. We tried flushing it out with warm water this morning, but it's not getting any better. I'm worried it might be something serious or that it'll spread to his other eye."

Conjunctivitis: Clinical presentation of conjunctival injection with purulent discharge in an 8 yo male is consistent with bacterial conjunctivitis. Normal corneal clarity and VA rule out keratitis or more serious ocular pathology, supporting uncomplicated conjunctivitis as primary diagnosis.

- [medication] Erythromycin Ophthalmic Ointment per package Apply 0.5 inch ribbon QID x 7 days - [education] Antibiotic drops for bacterial; supportive care for viral - [education] Good hand hygiene to prevent spread - [education] Do not share towels, pillowcases, or eye makeup - [education] May return to school 24 hours after starting treatment if bacterial - [education] Return if: vision changes, severe pain, or photophobia
Chronic Cough follow-up Chronic Cough, stable
- [medication] Continue current medication regimen - [follow-up] Return in 3-6 months for condition review
Obesity follow-up Obesity, stable
- [education] Nutrition counseling; goal of modest lifestyle changes - [referral] Refer to dietitian for comprehensive nutrition plan - [follow-up] Return in 3 months for weight check
Well-child examination - 8 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit
Obesity follow-up Obesity, stable
- [education] Nutrition counseling; goal of modest lifestyle changes - [referral] Refer to dietitian for comprehensive nutrition plan - [follow-up] Return in 3 months for weight check
Chronic Cough follow-up Chronic Cough, stable
- [medication] Continue current medication regimen - [follow-up] Return in 3-6 months for condition review
Chronic Cough follow-up Chronic Cough, stable
- [medication] Continue current medication regimen - [follow-up] Return in 3-6 months for condition review
Obesity follow-up Obesity, stable
- [education] Nutrition counseling; goal of modest lifestyle changes - [referral] Refer to dietitian for comprehensive nutrition plan - [follow-up] Return in 3 months for weight check

"He woke up yesterday morning with a fever and sore throat. We gave him some children's Tylenol and he seemed okay, but then today he's been complaining that his head hurts and he just won't eat anything. He's been running around some but gets tired pretty quick. We're mainly worried because the fever keeps coming back even after the medicine, and we want to make sure it's not strep or something serious."

Viral Syndrome: Clinical presentation consistent with viral syndrome: vital signs stable (HR 90), HEENT exam unremarkable without focal infection, and clear bilateral lung fields exclude bacterial pneumonia or acute otitis media. Absence of focal findings supports self-limited viral etiology.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days
Well-child examination - 7 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit
Chronic Cough follow-up Chronic Cough, stable
- [medication] Continue current medication regimen - [follow-up] Return in 3-6 months for condition review
Obesity follow-up Obesity, stable
- [education] Nutrition counseling; goal of modest lifestyle changes - [referral] Refer to dietitian for comprehensive nutrition plan - [follow-up] Return in 3 months for weight check
Obesity follow-up Obesity, stable
- [education] Nutrition counseling; goal of modest lifestyle changes - [referral] Refer to dietitian for comprehensive nutrition plan - [follow-up] Return in 3 months for weight check
Chronic Cough follow-up Chronic Cough, stable
- [medication] Continue current medication regimen - [follow-up] Return in 3-6 months for condition review

"He woke up yesterday morning complaining that his ear hurt and felt hot. By afternoon he had a fever and was really fussy and pulling at his ear, so we gave him some children's ibuprofen which helped a little bit. He wouldn't eat much for dinner and slept okay last night, but this morning his fever came back and he's still saying his ear hurts really bad. We're worried it might be infected and want to make sure he's okay."

Acute Otitis Media: Clinical presentation consistent with acute otitis media (AOM): fever, tachycardia, and classic otoscopic findings (erythematous, bulging TM with absent light reflex and decreased mobility) indicate middle ear inflammation with fluid accumulation. Absence of respiratory findings excludes concurrent pneumonia.

- [medication] Amoxicillin 1800mg divided BID x 10 days - [medication] Amoxicillin-Clavulanate 1800mg divided BID x 10 days - [medication] Ibuprofen 200mg Q6H PRN PRN - [education] Complete full course of antibiotics - [education] Ibuprofen or acetaminophen for pain control - [education] Return if no improvement in 48-72 hours - [education] Return immediately for high fever, facial swelling, or severe pain
Chronic Cough follow-up Chronic Cough, stable
- [medication] Continue current medication regimen - [follow-up] Return in 3-6 months for condition review
Well-child examination - 6 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit
Clavicle fracture
Obesity follow-up Obesity, stable
- [education] Nutrition counseling; goal of modest lifestyle changes - [referral] Refer to dietitian for comprehensive nutrition plan - [follow-up] Return in 3 months for weight check
Chronic Cough follow-up Chronic Cough, stable
- [medication] Continue current medication regimen - [follow-up] Return in 3-6 months for condition review

"His right eye started getting red and crusty yesterday morning when he woke up. Today it's gotten worse—there's a lot of yellow gunk coming out and he keeps rubbing it, saying it's itchy. We've been wiping it with a warm washcloth and keeping it clean, but it doesn't seem to be helping. I'm worried it might be an infection and want to make sure it clears up before he goes back to school."

Conjunctivitis: Clinical presentation of conjunctival injection with purulent discharge in a 5 yo male is consistent with bacterial conjunctivitis. Normal corneal clarity and VA rule out keratitis/more serious anterior segment pathology, supporting uncomplicated conjunctivitis diagnosis.

- [medication] Erythromycin Ophthalmic Ointment per package Apply 0.5 inch ribbon QID x 7 days - [education] Antibiotic drops for bacterial; supportive care for viral - [education] Good hand hygiene to prevent spread - [education] Do not share towels, pillowcases, or eye makeup - [education] May return to school 24 hours after starting treatment if bacterial - [education] Return if: vision changes, severe pain, or photophobia

"His left eye started getting red and watery yesterday morning. Today it's really crusty when he wakes up and he keeps rubbing it. There's some yellow stuff coming out of it. We tried wiping it with a warm cloth this morning, but it doesn't seem to be helping. I'm worried it might be an infection and want to make sure he can go back to school."

Conjunctivitis: History and exam findings of bilateral conjunctival injection with purulent discharge, eyelid edema, and preauricular lymphadenopathy are consistent with viral conjunctivitis. Normal cornea and VA exclude keratitis/corneal involvement, while clear lungs rule out systemic viral illness requiring further workup.

- [medication] Erythromycin Ophthalmic Ointment per package Apply 0.5 inch ribbon QID x 7 days - [education] Antibiotic drops for bacterial; supportive care for viral - [education] Good hand hygiene to prevent spread - [education] Do not share towels, pillowcases, or eye makeup - [education] May return to school 24 hours after starting treatment if bacterial - [education] Return if: vision changes, severe pain, or photophobia
Chronic Cough follow-up Chronic Cough, stable
- [medication] Continue current medication regimen - [follow-up] Return in 3-6 months for condition review
Obesity follow-up Obesity, stable
- [education] Nutrition counseling; goal of modest lifestyle changes - [referral] Refer to dietitian for comprehensive nutrition plan - [follow-up] Return in 3 months for weight check
Evaluation for obesity symptoms Obesity, newly diagnosed
- [education] Nutrition counseling; goal of modest lifestyle changes - [referral] Refer to dietitian for comprehensive nutrition plan - [follow-up] Return in 3 months for weight check

"He woke up yesterday morning with a fever and has been complaining that his throat hurts. He's been really tired and doesn't want to eat much, just drinking some juice. We gave him Tylenol this morning and it brought the fever down a little bit, but he's still not acting like himself. We're mainly worried because he's never been this sick before and we want to make sure it's nothing serious."

Viral Syndrome: Clinical presentation consistent w/ viral syndrome: normal vital signs (HR 88) & benign exam findings (normocephalic head, clear lungs bilaterally) r/o acute bacterial infection or respiratory compromise; absence of focal findings supports self-limited viral etiology.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days
Chronic Cough follow-up Chronic Cough, stable
- [medication] Continue current medication regimen - [follow-up] Return in 3-6 months for condition review
Well-child examination - 5 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

HPI (Parent Narrative): Benjamin started running a fever yesterday afternoon—his temperature got up to 101.5 at home. He's been complaining that his throat hurts and he's had a runny nose since this morning, so we think he picked something up at school. He's not eating much and seems pretty tired, just wants to lay around, but he's still drinking fluids okay. We gave him some children's ibuprofen yesterday which helped bring the fever down, but we wanted to get him checked out since it's been going on for more than 24 hours.

Viral Syndrome: Clinical presentation consistent w/ viral syndrome: mild tachycardia (HR 108) appropriate for age/fever, normal HEENT exam excludes bacterial sinusitis/otitis, and clear bilateral lung fields r/o lower respiratory involvement. Findings support self-limited viral etiology w/o evidence of bacterial superinfection or pneumonia.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days
Chronic Cough follow-up Chronic Cough, stable
- [medication] Continue current medication regimen - [follow-up] Return in 3-6 months for condition review

Benjamin started throwing up yesterday morning around 10 AM, and then had diarrhea a few hours later. He's had maybe five or six episodes of vomiting since then and his stools have been really loose and watery. He won't eat much of anything and just wants to drink, so we've been giving him small sips of water and Gatorade throughout the day. He seems pretty tired and fussy, and we're worried about him getting dehydrated since he's been going through diapers so fast.

Viral Gastroenteritis: Clinical presentation consistent w/ viral gastroenteritis: mild tachycardia (HR 109) suggests possible mild dehydration from GI losses, while normal HEENT and clear lungs exclude alternative diagnoses (meningitis, pneumonia) and support primary GI etiology.

- [medication] Ondansetron 2mg x1 dose PRN PRN - [education] Oral rehydration with small frequent amounts (1-2 tsp every 5 minutes) - [education] Use Pedialyte or similar oral rehydration solution - [education] Advance diet as tolerated - no need for BRAT restriction - [education] Return if: unable to keep fluids down, no urine in 8 hours, blood in stool, or appears very ill

"He woke up this morning with a bunch of red spots on his chest and back. They weren't there last night when I put him to bed. The rash is spread all over now and he's been really itchy—he keeps scratching at it. He's acting normal otherwise, playing and everything, but I'm worried it might be contagious or something serious. I gave him some Benadryl cream this afternoon but it doesn't seem to be helping much."

Rash: Clinical presentation of rash with normal vital signs (HR 101 appropriate for age), clear lungs, and benign HEENT exam supports uncomplicated dermatologic process without systemic involvement or respiratory compromise.
- [other] Topical care as appropriate for rash type - [education] Return if rash spreads, becomes painful, or child develops fever
Chronic Cough follow-up Chronic Cough, stable
- [medication] Continue current medication regimen - [follow-up] Return in 3-6 months for condition review
Chronic Cough follow-up Chronic Cough, stable
- [medication] Continue current medication regimen - [follow-up] Return in 3-6 months for condition review
Well-child examination - 4 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"He had a scraped knee from playing outside about a week ago, and it seemed to be healing fine. But then starting yesterday morning, we noticed some crusty, yellowish sores popping up around the scrape and now on his arm too. They look kind of oozy and he's been scratching at them a lot even though we keep telling him not to. We've been washing them with soap and water and putting antibiotic ointment on, but they just seem to be spreading. I'm worried it's getting infected and I want to make sure it doesn't get worse."

Impetigo: Clinical presentation of localized pustules/crusted lesions on exposed skin areas in a 3 yo is classic for impetigo. Absence of systemic toxicity (normal HR, clear lungs) suggests non-bullous impetigo without complications.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days
Chronic Cough follow-up Chronic Cough, stable
- [medication] Continue current medication regimen - [follow-up] Return in 3-6 months for condition review
Chronic Cough follow-up Chronic Cough, stable
- [medication] Continue current medication regimen - [follow-up] Return in 3-6 months for condition review

"He woke up yesterday morning with a fever and has been really fussy ever since. He's been complaining that his head and body hurt, and he won't eat much—just had some juice this morning. We gave him some children's Tylenol and a cool bath to try to bring the fever down, but it keeps coming back. We're worried because he's so tired and clingy, and we're not sure if this is just a cold or something worse."

Influenza: Clinical presentation consistent with influenza: vital signs stable (HR 99 bpm, age-appropriate), normal HEENT exam excludes complications (otitis media, sinusitis), and clear lung fields bilaterally rule out secondary bacterial pneumonia at this time.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days
Well-child examination - 3 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"He woke up yesterday morning with a fever. We checked his temperature and it was 102, so we gave him some Tylenol and he seemed to feel a little better for a few hours. But then the fever came back this afternoon and it's been staying around 101 to 102 even with the medicine. He's been pretty fussy and won't eat much, just wants to drink. We're mainly worried because he's never had a fever this high before and we want to make sure it's nothing serious."

Fever: History and exam consistent w/ fever; vital signs show mild tachycardia (HR 92) appropriate for elevated temp, while normal HEENT and clear lungs bilaterally help exclude acute focal infection (otitis, pneumonia) as primary etiology at this time.
- [medication] Acetaminophen or ibuprofen for temperature control - [education] Monitor for source of infection, return if fever persists >3 days - [follow-up] Follow up as needed if symptoms worsen
Chronic Cough follow-up Chronic Cough, stable
- [medication] Continue current medication regimen - [follow-up] Return in 3-6 months for condition review

"His right eye started getting red and crusty yesterday morning. This morning there was a lot of yellow gunk coming out of it and it seems to be bothering him—he keeps rubbing at it and his eye is pretty swollen. We tried wiping it with a warm washcloth a few times but it's not getting any better. We're worried it might be an infection and want to make sure it's not something serious."

Conjunctivitis: History and exam reveal classic signs of conjunctivitis: bilateral conjunctival injection with purulent discharge on HEENT exam, with preserved visual acuity and clear cornea ruling out more serious anterior segment pathology. Tachycardia (HR 109) likely represents normal response to discomfort/fever rather than systemic involvement, and clear lungs make concurrent respiratory infection less likely.

- [medication] Erythromycin Ophthalmic Ointment per package Apply 0.5 inch ribbon QID x 7 days - [education] Antibiotic drops for bacterial; supportive care for viral - [education] Good hand hygiene to prevent spread - [education] Do not share towels, pillowcases, or eye makeup - [education] May return to school 24 hours after starting treatment if bacterial - [education] Return if: vision changes, severe pain, or photophobia
Well-child examination - 2 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit
Chronic Cough follow-up Chronic Cough, stable
- [medication] Continue current medication regimen - [follow-up] Return in 3-6 months for condition review

"He woke up yesterday morning with a really runny nose and has been sneezing a lot today. His nose is so stuffy that he's having trouble eating and seems pretty fussy about it. We gave him some saline drops this morning and it helped a little bit, but the sniffles keep coming back. We're worried it might turn into an ear infection or something worse since he's been tugging at his ears."

Allergic Rhinitis: Clinical presentation consistent with allergic rhinitis: nasal symptoms with normal HEENT exam (no acute infection signs). Vital signs elevated (HR 123, RR 28) likely reflect discomfort/irritability rather than systemic illness, with clear lungs ruling out lower respiratory involvement.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days

Benjamin started running a fever yesterday morning—I checked his temperature and it was 102.5. He's been really fussy and clingy, won't eat much, and has had a runny nose and cough since this morning. I gave him some children's acetaminophen and kept him home from daycare, but he's still pretty uncomfortable and I'm worried the fever will get worse.

Influenza: Clinical presentation consistent w/ influenza: tachycardia (HR 124) reflects systemic viral infection and fever; normal lung exam excludes pneumonia/lower respiratory involvement, supporting uncomplicated influenza.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days
Chronic Cough follow-up Chronic Cough, stable
- [medication] Continue current medication regimen - [follow-up] Return in 3-6 months for condition review

HPI (Parent Narrative): Benjamin woke up yesterday morning with a runny nose and has been pretty fussy ever since. He's had a fever on and off—we checked it this morning and got 101.2. He's not eating much, just picking at his food, and he's been coughing a little bit, especially when he lies down. We gave him some children's ibuprofen and tried keeping him hydrated, but he's still pretty uncomfortable and we're worried the fever might get worse.

Upper Respiratory Infection: Clinical presentation consistent with URI: fever (101.2°F), mild pharyngeal erythema, and appropriate vital sign elevation (HR 105, RR 25) without signs of lower respiratory involvement (clear lung fields bilaterally). Absence of exudate and clear lungs help exclude bacterial pharyngitis and pneumonia.

- [medication] Acetaminophen 180mg Q4-6H PRN PRN - [medication] Ibuprofen 120mg Q6-8H PRN PRN - [education] Supportive care with rest and increased fluids - [education] Saline nasal drops and bulb suction for congestion - [education] Honey (if >1 year) for cough relief - [education] Return if: difficulty breathing, fever >3 days, ear pain, or worsening symptoms
Well-child examination - 2 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit
Chronic Cough follow-up Chronic Cough, stable
- [medication] Continue current medication regimen - [follow-up] Return in 3-6 months for condition review

"He woke up yesterday morning with a runny nose and has had a low fever since then—we checked it this morning and it was about 100.8. He's been pretty fussy and doesn't want to eat much, mostly just drinking his milk. We've been giving him cool baths and some children's pain reliever, but the runny nose just keeps going. We're mainly worried because he's never had a fever like this before and we want to make sure it's nothing serious."

Upper Respiratory Infection: Clinical presentation consistent with URI: clear rhinorrhea and erythematous nasal mucosa indicate viral upper airway inflammation, supported by low-grade fever (100.8°F) and mild tachycardia (HR 122) typical of viral infection in toddlers; clear lung fields exclude lower respiratory involvement.

- [medication] Acetaminophen 170mg Q4-6H PRN PRN - [medication] Ibuprofen 110mg Q6-8H PRN PRN - [education] Supportive care with rest and increased fluids - [education] Saline nasal drops and bulb suction for congestion - [education] Honey (if >1 year) for cough relief - [education] Return if: difficulty breathing, fever >3 days, ear pain, or worsening symptoms
Evaluation for chronic cough symptoms Chronic Cough, newly diagnosed
- [medication] Continue current medication regimen - [follow-up] Return in 3-6 months for condition review
Well-child examination - 1 year 6 month old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

Benjamin started running a fever yesterday afternoon—we noticed he felt warm and took his temperature, which was 101.5. He's been pretty fussy and clingy all day, and he won't eat his usual meals, though he's been drinking some water and juice. This morning he developed a runny nose and a little cough. We gave him acetaminophen yesterday which helped bring the fever down temporarily, but it came back. We're mainly worried because he just seems really uncomfortable and we want to make sure it's not anything serious.

Viral Syndrome: Clinical presentation consistent with viral syndrome: mild tachycardia (HR 108) and tachypnea (RR 29) are expected compensatory responses to systemic viral infection in toddlers; normal HEENT and clear lungs bilaterally exclude focal bacterial infection or pneumonia.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days

"He started with a runny nose two days ago, and now he's got a lot of clear mucus coming out. He's been pretty fussy and won't eat as much as usual, mostly just drinking his milk. We've been using the saline drops at home and a humidifier in his room at night, but the runny nose just won't stop. I'm mainly worried it's going to turn into an ear infection or something worse like last time."

Upper Respiratory Infection: Clinical presentation consistent with URI: clear rhinorrhea and erythematous nasal mucosa indicate viral upper airway inflammation, while clear bilateral lung fields and mild tachycardia/tachypnea (likely compensatory) exclude lower respiratory involvement and systemic toxicity.

- [medication] Acetaminophen 160mg Q4-6H PRN PRN - [medication] Ibuprofen 110mg Q6-8H PRN PRN - [education] Supportive care with rest and increased fluids - [education] Saline nasal drops and bulb suction for congestion - [education] Honey (if >1 year) for cough relief - [education] Return if: difficulty breathing, fever >3 days, ear pain, or worsening symptoms
Well-child examination - 1 year 3 month old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

Benjamin started running a fever yesterday afternoon - it got up to 101.5 at home. He's been really fussy and clingy, won't eat much, and has had a runny nose and cough since this morning. We gave him some acetaminophen which brought the fever down a bit, but he's just not himself. We're worried because he seems uncomfortable and we're not sure if we should be doing anything else at home.

Influenza: Clinical presentation consistent with influenza: Vital sign abnormalities (HR 124, RR 25) reflect systemic viral infection and compensatory tachycardia/tachypnea, while clear lung fields on exam exclude lower respiratory tract involvement at this time, supporting uncomplicated influenza in this 14-month-old.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days
Well-child examination - 1 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

HPI - Benjamin, 11 months old "He woke up yesterday morning with a runny nose and has been pretty fussy ever since. This morning I noticed he felt warm, so I took his temperature and got 100.9. He's been sneezing a lot and has clear mucus coming out of his nose, but he's still drinking his bottles okay. I gave him some acetaminophen about two hours ago and he seemed a little better, but I'm worried because he's not his normal self and I want to make sure it's nothing serious."

Upper Respiratory Infection: Clinical presentation consistent with viral URI: rhinorrhea with mild nasal mucositis, pharyngeal erythema without exudate, and clear lungs exclude bacterial infection or lower respiratory involvement. Vital sign abnormalities (fever, tachycardia, tachypnea) are expected physiologic response to viral infection in this age group.

- [medication] Acetaminophen 140mg Q4-6H PRN PRN - [medication] Ibuprofen 90mg Q6-8H PRN PRN - [education] Supportive care with rest and increased fluids - [education] Saline nasal drops and bulb suction for congestion - [education] Honey (if >1 year) for cough relief - [education] Return if: difficulty breathing, fever >3 days, ear pain, or worsening symptoms
Well-child examination - 9 month old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

Benjamin's mom reports he developed some red, crusty spots on his left cheek about 3 days ago. She initially thought it was just a rash from drooling, but the spots have gotten bigger and started oozing a yellowish fluid over the past day. She tried keeping the area clean with warm water and applied some Aquaphor, but it's spreading to his chin now. She's concerned it might be infected and worried about it spreading to his diaper area.

Impetigo: Clinical presentation of localized pustules/crusted lesions consistent w/ impetigo, a superficial bacterial skin infection common in infants. Vital signs show mild tachycardia/tachypnea likely 2° to infection/fever, while normal HEENT & clear lungs exclude systemic complications or alternative diagnoses.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days

HPI: Benjamin started getting a runny nose about 3 days ago, and yesterday he developed a cough. He's been sneezing a lot and seems more fussy than usual, especially at bedtime. He's still eating okay but not as much as he normally does, and we've been using the bulb syringe to clear his nose a few times a day. We're mainly worried because this is his first real cold and we want to make sure it doesn't turn into something worse.

Upper Respiratory Infection: Clinical presentation consistent with viral URI: clear rhinorrhea with mild nasal mucosa erythema, mild oropharyngeal erythema without exudate, and clear bilateral lung fields. Tachycardia (HR 139) and tachypnea (RR 36) are expected physiologic responses to viral infection in this age group without evidence of lower respiratory involvement or bacterial superinfection.

- [medication] Acetaminophen 110mg Q4-6H PRN PRN - [medication] Ibuprofen 75mg Q6-8H PRN PRN - [education] Supportive care with rest and increased fluids - [education] Saline nasal drops and bulb suction for congestion - [education] Honey (if >1 year) for cough relief - [education] Return if: difficulty breathing, fever >3 days, ear pain, or worsening symptoms
Well-child examination - 5 month old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit
Well-child examination - 4 month old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"He's had a fever since yesterday morning. We first noticed he felt warm, and when we took his temperature it was 101.5. He's been fussier than usual and doesn't seem interested in his bottles like he normally does. We gave him some infant acetaminophen this morning and it came down a bit, but it keeps coming back. We're worried because he's so young and we're not sure if this is serious."

Fever: History and exam consistent w/ fever: elevated HR (105) and RR (27) reflect appropriate physiologic response to elevated core temp. Normal HEENT and clear lungs help exclude acute focal infection (otitis, pneumonia) as primary etiology, though further workup (labs, imaging) warranted given age <3 mo and fever.
- [medication] Acetaminophen or ibuprofen for temperature control - [education] Monitor for source of infection, return if fever persists >3 days - [follow-up] Follow up as needed if symptoms worsen

"He's had a runny nose for about two days now—started with just clear stuff coming out, and yesterday he got a little stuffy. He's been fussier than usual and doesn't seem as interested in his bottles, though he's still taking some. We've been using the bulb syringe to clear his nose and keeping him elevated, but we're worried because he's so young. He doesn't have a fever, but we wanted to make sure it's nothing serious."

Upper Respiratory Infection: Clinical presentation consistent with URI: clear rhinorrhea with mild nasal mucosa erythema, oropharyngeal erythema without exudate, and clear lung fields support viral etiology. Mild tachycardia (HR 118) and tachypnea (RR 32) are appropriate compensatory responses to URI in 3-month-old without evidence of lower respiratory involvement or respiratory distress.

- [medication] Acetaminophen 80mg Q4-6H PRN PRN - [education] Supportive care with rest and increased fluids - [education] Saline nasal drops and bulb suction for congestion - [education] Honey (if >1 year) for cough relief - [education] Return if: difficulty breathing, fever >3 days, ear pain, or worsening symptoms
Well-child examination - 2 month old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit
Well-child examination - 1 month old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit
